court: High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh

compensation_breakdown.notes: Compensation of Rs.11,76,509/- awarded in the claim petition under Section 166 M.V. Act; High Court noted this award and maintained order for grant under Section 357(3) Cr.P.C.

facts: On 17.11.2016 at about 7:30 P.M. in Kabrel/Hisar a pick-up van bearing registration No. HR 44E-0207 driven rashly struck and injured Balbir near a roadside khokha; the vehicle then rammed into the stall and was abandoned. Balbir was hospitalized at multiple hospitals and died on 09.12.2016 from injuries. FIR No. 258 dated 18.11.2016 was registered under Sections 279, 337, 304-A, 427 IPC and Sections 181, 192 MV Act; the accused Sanjay was arrested and tried.
arguments_claimant: The petitioner argued the death was not the proximate result of the accident because the deceased left medical treatment repeatedly (LAMA) and was shifted between hospitals, creating reasonable doubt about causation. He also contended that identification was infirm because no Test Identification Parade was conducted and key witnesses first identified the accused in court and are closely related to the deceased.
arguments_respondent: State counsel argued the deceased remained under continuous treatment until death, the medical and post-mortem evidence links death to the accident, the vehicle's mechanical damage corroborates the collision, the petitioner failed to explain who else could have been driving, and the family had been awarded compensation (Rs.11,76,509/-) which militated against leniency.
ratio_decidendi: "The legal position is well established that when a driver is operating a vehicle in a crowded or busy locality, the standard of care required is considerably higher, and any deviation from that duty amounts to negligence. Driving in a rash and negligent manner in a busy area enhances culpability." The Court held that the prosecution proved "rashness and negligence on part of the accused" on the basis of consistent ocular evidence, site plan and mechanical report. The Court further held that the absence of a Test Identification Parade does not ipso facto entitle the accused to the benefit of doubt and relied on the principle that "identification of the accused in court is valid and admissible in law" (Ravi Kapur). Finally, the Court ruled that where injuries are grievous and the post-mortem "clearly attributes the cause of death to complications arising from the injuries sustained", discontinuance of medical treatment (LAMA) does not break the proximate link between accident and death.
final_order: Criminal revision petition partly allowed: the conviction affirmed and appeal dismissed; sentences under Sections 279, 337, 427 IPC remain unchanged; sentence under Section 304-A IPC reduced from RI for 2 years to RI for 18 months with fine of Rs.3,000/- (default SI one month); bail and surety bonds cancelled and accused to be taken into custody; order for grant of compensation under Section 357(3) Cr.P.C. is maintained.
summary: This is a criminal revision in a 2016 motor-accident death case (High Court of Punjab & Haryana, 13.10.2025). The accused driver Sanjay was convicted for rash/negligent driving causing death (Sections 279, 337, 304-A, 427 IPC; MV Act Ss.181,192). The High Court upheld the convictions and evidence (including in-court identification without TIP and medical/post-mortem linkage), but reduced the 304-A sentence from two years to 18 months, noting that the family had received MV Act compensation of Rs.11,76,509/-. The decision confirms principles on higher duty of care in crowded localities, admissibility of court identification, and causation despite LAMA.
embedding_summary: Sanjay v. State of Haryana, High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh, CRR-2406-2025 (O&M), decision 13.10.2025. Facts: 17.11.2016 pick-up van (Reg. No. HR 44E-0207) struck and killed Balbir at Kabrel/Hisar; FIR under Sections 279, 337, 304-A, 427 IPC and Sections 181, 192 MV Act. Issues: identity without TIP, proximate causation where injured left treatment LAMA, standard of care in crowded locality, sentencing and mitigation given prior MV Act compensation. Court relied on Ravi Kapur (court identification), Mohammed Aynuddin (criminal negligence/rashness), Soman v. State of Kerala and Balwinder Singh on sentencing principles. Outcome: convictions affirmed; 304-A sentence reduced from 2 years RI to 18 months RI; compensation of Rs.11,76,509/- awarded earlier to family under Section 166 M.V. Act was noted and Section 357(3) CrPC order maintained.
legal_principles: Higher standard of care in crowded or busy locality increases driver's culpability; deviation amounts to negligence.
Identification in-court is admissible and absence of a Test Identification Parade does not ipso facto entitle accused to benefit of doubt (relying on Ravi Kapur).
Where injuries are grievous and post-mortem attributes death to those injuries, discontinuance of medical treatment (LAMA) does not break proximate causation.
Res ipsa loquitur may apply where vehicle's conduct and collision circumstances indicate negligence.
Sentencing must balance proportionality and deterrence; prior award of compensation under M.V. Act may be a mitigating factor.
cases_cited: Ravi Kapur v. State of Rajasthan, [2012 AIR (SC) 2986]
Mohammed Aynuddin @ Miyam vs State of Andhra, 2000(3) RCR (Crl.) 619
State of Punjab v. Balwinder Singh & Others, (2012) 2 SCC 182
Soman vs State of Kerala, (2013) 11 SCC 382
State through Central Bureau of Investigation, Anti Corruption Branch, Chandigarh Vs. Sanjiv Bhalla and another
State of Punjab v. Prem Sagar, (2008) 7 SCC 550
bm25_text: Sanjay vs State Of Haryana High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh 2025 On 17.11.2016 at about 7:30 P.M. in Kabrel/Hisar a pick-up van bearing registration No. HR 44E-0207 driven rashly struck and injured Balbir near a roadside khokha; the vehicle then rammed into the stall and was abandoned. Balbir was hospitalized at multiple hospitals and died on 09.12.2016 from injuries. FIR No. 258 dated 18.11.2016 was registered under Sections 279, 337, 304-A, 427 IPC and Sections 181, 192 MV Act; the accused Sanjay was arrested and tried. The petitioner argued the death was not the proximate result of the accident because the deceased left medical treatment repeatedly (LAMA) and was shifted between hospitals, creating reasonable doubt about causation. He also contended that identification was infirm because no Test Identification Parade was conducted and key witnesses first identified the accused in court and are closely related to the deceased. State counsel argued the deceased remained under continuous treatment until death, the medical and post-mortem evidence links death to the accident, the vehicle's mechanical damage corroborates the collision, the petitioner failed to explain who else could have been driving, and the family had been awarded compensation (Rs.11,76,509/-) which militated against leniency. "The legal position is well established that when a driver is operating a vehicle in a crowded or busy locality, the standard of care required is considerably higher, and any deviation from that duty amounts to negligence. Driving in a rash and negligent manner in a busy area enhances culpability." The Court held that the prosecution proved "rashness and negligence on part of the accused" on the basis of consistent ocular evidence, site plan and mechanical report. The Court further held that the absence of a Test Identification Parade does not ipso facto entitle the accused to the benefit of doubt and relied on the principle that "identification of the accused in court is valid and admissible in law" (Ravi Kapur). Finally, the Court ruled that where injuries are grievous and the post-mortem "clearly attributes the cause of death to complications arising from the injuries sustained", discontinuance of medical treatment (LAMA) does not break the proximate link between accident and death. Criminal revision petition partly allowed: the conviction affirmed and appeal dismissed; sentences under Sections 279, 337, 427 IPC remain unchanged; sentence under Section 304-A IPC reduced from RI for 2 years to RI for 18 months with fine of Rs.3,000/- (default SI one month); bail and surety bonds cancelled and accused to be taken into custody; order for grant of compensation under Section 357(3) Cr.P.C. is maintained. This is a criminal revision in a 2016 motor-accident death case (High Court of Punjab & Haryana, 13.10.2025). The accused driver Sanjay was convicted for rash/negligent driving causing death (Sections 279, 337, 304-A, 427 IPC; MV Act Ss.181,192). The High Court upheld the convictions and evidence (including in-court identification without TIP and medical/post-mortem linkage), but reduced the 304-A sentence from two years to 18 months, noting that the family had received MV Act compensation of Rs.11,76,509/-. The decision confirms principles on higher duty of care in crowded localities, admissibility of court identification, and causation despite LAMA. Sanjay v. State of Haryana, High Court of Punjab & Haryana at Chandigarh, CRR-2406-2025 (O&M), decision 13.10.2025. Facts: 17.11.2016 pick-up van (Reg. No. HR 44E-0207) struck and killed Balbir at Kabrel/Hisar; FIR under Sections 279, 337, 304-A, 427 IPC and Sections 181, 192 MV Act. Issues: identity without TIP, proximate causation where injured left treatment LAMA, standard of care in crowded locality, sentencing and mitigation given prior MV Act compensation. Court relied on Ravi Kapur (court identification), Mohammed Aynuddin (criminal negligence/rashness), Soman v. State of Kerala and Balwinder Singh on sentencing principles. Outcome: convictions affirmed; 304-A sentence reduced from 2 years RI to 18 months RI; compensation of Rs.11,76,509/- awarded earlier to family under Section 166 M.V. Higher standard of care in crowded or busy locality increases driver's culpability; deviation amounts to negligence. Identification in-court is admissible and absence of a Test Identification Parade does not ipso facto entitle accused to benefit of doubt (relying on Ravi Kapur). Where injuries are grievous and post-mortem attributes death to those injuries, discontinuance of medical treatment (LAMA) does not break proximate causation. Res ipsa loquitur may apply where vehicle's conduct and collision circumstances indicate negligence. Sentencing must balance proportionality and deterrence; prior award of compensation under M.V. Act may be a mitigating factor.
